assertions.text: Discount ladder, and traveller eligibility are in separate tables. Rolling window counting as a third table is desirable.
assertions.evidence: The response produces two tables: one for children's flat discount and one for the adult/senior tier ladder. There is no eligibility table separating who qualifies for what (children vs adults/seniors vs seniors). Eligibility is implicitly embedded in the table structure by having separate tables per type, not as a dedicated concern. No rolling window table is present.

assertions.text: Children's flat 20% discount is represented — either as a row in the eligibility table or a separate note. It does not follow the ladder.
assertions.evidence: A dedicated table `childDiscountIsAlwaysFlat20Percent` shows `Child, flat rate regardless | {0, 5, 20, 40} | 20`, clearly representing the flat 20% and noting it is 'regardless' of trip count. The @Description states 'Children always receive a flat 20% discount on single tickets, regardless of how many tickets they have purchased recently.'

assertions.text: The discount ladder has multiple tiers derived from the 'every fifth trip' rule, with boundary values or value sets showing which ticket counts produce which discount. The maximum 40% discount is represented.
assertions.evidence: The ladder table includes rows at 0, 4, 5, 9, 10, 15, 20, 39, 40, 50 mapping to 0%, 0%, 5%, 5%, 10%, 15%, 20%, 35%, 40%, 40% respectively, covering multiple tiers and the maximum 40% cap.

assertions.text: The 30-day rolling window boundary is tested: a ticket at exactly 30 days is included, at 31 days is excluded.
assertions.evidence: The response does not include any table or test rows for the 30-day rolling window boundary. The spec ambiguity note only discusses whether the current ticket is included in the count, not the 30-day cutoff boundary.

assertions.text: Eligibility uses human-readable values (yes/no or equivalent) rather than raw true/false. TypeConverter or similar mechanism mentioned.
assertions.evidence: No eligibility table with yes/no values is present. The tables use numeric discount percentages and CustomerType enum values. No TypeConverter or human-readable eligibility conversion is mentioned.

assertions.text: Value sets in the tier table contain exactly the values that produce the same discount. No cross-tier contamination.
assertions.evidence: In the child table, `{0, 5, 20, 40}` all map to 20% which is correct. In the adult/senior table, `{ADULT, SENIOR}` is used for customer type (not ticket count), and individual ticket count rows are separate — no value set spans multiple discount tiers.

assertions.text: The discount ladder table uses value sets for the ticket count column to group counts within the same tier (e.g. {5, 6, 7, 8, 9} or {5, 9} for min/max → 5%) — not one row per boundary value. The 'every fifth trip' rule creates tiers; the table should express tiers, not enumerate boundaries.
assertions.evidence: The adult/senior table uses individual boundary rows (4, 5, 9, 10, 15, 20, 39, 40, 50) rather than value sets grouping each tier. For example, tier 1 (5%) is represented by two separate rows `| 5 | 5` and `| 9 | 5` instead of `| {5, 9} | 5`.

assertions.text: The tier-to-discount mapping is expressed once — not repeated across multiple tables. A price application table (if present) should test the arithmetic (base price × discount → final price) without re-enumerating which ticket counts map to which tier.
assertions.evidence: The tier-to-discount mapping appears in only one table (`adultAndSeniorAccumulateReisDiscountByTripFrequency`). There is no second table that re-enumerates the same tier mappings, and no price application table is present.

assertions.text: @DisplayName is present on test methods, OR method names read as clear descriptive titles when converted from camelCase/snake_case.
assertions.evidence: `@DisplayName("Reis single-ticket discount")` is present on the class. Method names `childDiscountIsAlwaysFlat20Percent` and `adultAndSeniorAccumulateReisDiscountByTripFrequency` read as clear descriptive titles in camelCase.

assertions.text: @Description is present on at least the class or the tier method and provides context beyond what the table rows already express — such as the discount ladder structure, eligibility rules, or application context. Does NOT merely restate the column names or summarise what the rows show.
assertions.evidence: Both methods have @Description. The tier method's description explains the business rule: 'Every 5 trips moves one step up the discount ladder (+5%). The first discount applies at the 5th trip; the maximum of 40% is reached at 40 trips and is never exceeded.' This contextualizes the ladder structure beyond what the rows express.

assertions.text: TypeConverterSources is used for at least one human-readable conversion (yes/no, or rolling window notation).
assertions.evidence: No TypeConverterSources annotation or TypeConverter is present anywhere in the response. All values use numeric and enum types directly.

assertions.text: Annotations appear in order: @DisplayName (if present), @Description (if present), @TableTest — not any other order.
assertions.evidence: On the class: `@DisplayName` appears before the class declaration. On each method: `@Description` appears before `@TableTest`. The order `@Description` then `@TableTest` is followed consistently for both test methods.

assertions.text: @Description text uses a text block (triple-quoted string """) when the text is long, not string concatenation with +.
assertions.evidence: Both @Description annotations use string concatenation with `+`: `@Description("Children always receive..." + "regardless of how many...")` and the tier method similarly uses `+ "in the last 30 days..."` etc. — not triple-quoted text blocks.
